insights.title: Insights on Early Childhood Development Index 2030
insights.subtitle: AI-generated insights based on National Demographic and Health Survey data
insights.overview: Nationally, the early childhood development index 2030 rose from 76.8 in 2022 to 78.3 in 2025, but that average sits on top of very wide regional differences in 2025. BARMM is at 47.2 while several regions are above 80, including Cagayan Valley at 92.2 and Negros Island Region at 93.0 (with no 2022 estimate for NIR). The core story is therefore not just modest national improvement, but a strongly uneven regional profile where children in some regions are far less likely to be developmentally on-track than children in others.

sections.heading: Regional spread is the dominant pattern
sections.body: The 2025 range is large: from 47.2 in BARMM to 93.0 in Negros Island Region and 92.2 in Cagayan Valley. Even excluding NIR (which has no 2022 value), the spread remains substantial, with multiple regions in the low 80s and upper 70s while BARMM remains far below the rest. This means national movement of 1.5 points can mask much larger place-based differences in children's developmental outcomes.

sections.heading: Regional change from 2022 to 2025 is uneven, not one-directional
sections.body: Several regions improved strongly, including Bicol (73.2 to 84.4), SOCCSKSARGEN (71.9 to 81.7), Northern Mindanao (76.2 to 82.9), NCR (73.9 to 81.0), and Cagayan Valley (83.2 to 92.2). At the same time, some regions declined materially, notably Zamboanga Peninsula (81.1 to 67.3), Eastern Visayas (85.0 to 77.2), Central Luzon (84.8 to 78.9), and Ilocos Region (85.2 to 79.8). The regional map is therefore a reshuffling, not a uniform national climb.

sections.heading: High and low regions are structurally different contexts
sections.body: In 2025, high-performing regions cluster at 80 and above across several major areas of Luzon, Visayas, and Mindanao, while BARMM remains a clear outlier at 47.2. Zamboanga Peninsula at 67.3 is also distinctly below the upper cluster. This gap is large enough that policy interpretation should separate low-performing regional contexts from the national average instead of treating all regions as small deviations around one center.

sections.heading: Sex and residence differences are visible but smaller than regional gaps
sections.body: Girls are higher than boys in 2025 (83.3 vs 73.9), and the female-male gap widened relative to 2022 (79.6 vs 74.3). Urban children are higher than rural children (80.4 vs 76.0), with both improving since 2022 (urban 78.1, rural 75.4). These differences matter, but they are still much smaller than the 40+ point spread between the highest and lowest regions.

sections.heading: Age profile is mixed, with the oldest group showing the largest recent gain
sections.body: Children aged 24-35 months and 36-47 months are relatively close in 2025 (81.9 and 77.8), while 48-59 months is lower at 75.1. Compared with 2022, the largest improvement is in the oldest group (69.4 to 75.1), while ages 36-47 declined (79.8 to 77.8) and ages 24-35 were nearly flat (81.6 to 81.9). This is a non-linear age pattern rather than a simple monotonic trend.

sections.heading: Education and wealth patterns are strong but not strictly linear
sections.body: By mother's education, 2025 values are lowest for no education (59.1) and Grades 1-6 (61.4), while Grades 11-12 (88.2) and college (84.6) are much higher. By wealth, the lowest quintile is at 70.9 versus 85.0 in the highest quintile. But the middle quintile (75.4) is below the second quintile (81.2), showing a non-linear gradient. Attendance to early childhood education is also strongly associated with higher values (83.4 among attendees vs 73.3 among never attended).

takeaways: National ECDI 2030 improved modestly from 2022 to 2025, but regional inequality remains wide and structurally important.
Regional trajectories diverge: several regions improved strongly while others fell, so national gains should not be read as uniform progress.
BARMM remains a clear low outlier, while high-performing regions cluster above 80, indicating very different regional environments for early child development.
Sex, residence, education, wealth, and early education attendance all matter, but their gaps are generally smaller than the largest regional divides.
